writing.task1: The chart illustrates the percentage of households in owned and rented accommodation in England and Wales from 1918 to 2011. Overall, there was a dramatic shift from rented to owned accommodation over this period. In 1918, the vast majority of households (77%) lived in rented accommodation, while only 23% owned their homes. This trend continued until the mid-20th century when homeownership began to increase significantly. By 2011, the situation had completely reversed, with the majority of households now owning their accommodation. This transformation reflects major social and economic changes, including government policies promoting homeownership, increased prosperity, and changing attitudes towards property ownership. The shift from rented to owned accommodation represents one of the most significant social changes in British housing history over the past century.
writing.task2: The question of whether acting is a natural talent or a learned skill has been debated for centuries. While some argue that great actors are born with innate abilities, others believe that acting can be developed through proper training and practice. Both perspectives have merit and the truth likely lies somewhere in between. Those who support the natural talent argument point to child prodigies and actors who seem to possess an intuitive understanding of human emotions and behavior. These individuals often display remarkable abilities from a young age, suggesting that some people are naturally gifted performers. However, even the most talented actors benefit from training and experience. Acting involves complex skills including voice projection, movement, emotional expression, and character development. These techniques can be taught and refined through practice. Furthermore, the acting profession requires discipline, memorization, and the ability to work collaboratively with others. These are skills that must be learned and developed over time. In conclusion, while natural talent may provide a foundation, acting is ultimately a craft that requires both innate ability and dedicated training to master successfully.

testData.testTitle: IELTS Acting and Theater Practice Test

sections.title: Listening
sections.description: 40 questions • Approximately 30 minutes • You will hear each recording only once

parts.title: Part 1: Conversation about drama course enrollment
parts.instructions: Listen to the conversation and answer questions 1-10.

script.narrator:
Good morning, this is the International English Language Testing System, IELTS. You will hear a number of different recordings and you will have to answer questions on what you hear. There will be time for you to read the instructions and questions and you will have a chance to check your work. All the recordings will be played once only. The test is in four parts. At the end of the test you will be given ten minutes to transfer your answers to an answer sheet.

Now turn to Part 1. You will hear a conversation between a student and a drama course coordinator. Look at questions 1 to 10. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 10.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Good morning, Royal Academy of Dramatic Arts. How can I help you?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Hello, I'm interested in enrolling in your acting course for beginners.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Certainly! Our beginner's course runs for eight weeks, starting on September 15th.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Perfect. What's the schedule like?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Classes are every Tuesday and Thursday from 6 PM to 9 PM. The course fee is £480, which includes all materials.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: That sounds reasonable. Could you tell me what the course covers?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Yes, we focus on voice projection, character development, improvisation, and basic stage techniques. The final week includes a small performance for friends and family.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Wonderful! My name is James Wilson, that's J-A-M-E-S W-I-L-S-O-N.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Thank you, Mr. Wilson. And your phone number?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Got it. The instructor is Sarah Johnson - she's been teaching here for five years. You'll need to bring comfortable clothing and a notebook.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Are there any age restrictions?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Participants must be at least 16 years old. The maximum class size is 15 students.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: That's great. I'll definitely enroll. Thank you for your help.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: You're welcome, Mr. Wilson. We look forward to seeing you in class.

questions.questionText: What is the man's name?
questions.translation: Tên của người đàn ông là gì?

questions.questionText: Where is the conversation taking place?
questions.translation: Cuộc hội thoại diễn ra ở đâu?

questions.questionText: How long does the course last?
questions.translation: Khóa học kéo dài bao lâu?

questions.questionText: What is the course fee?
questions.translation: Học phí khóa học là bao nhiêu?

questions.questionText: Which skill is NOT mentioned in the course?
questions.translation: Kỹ năng nào KHÔNG được đề cập trong khóa học?

questions.questionText: What is the phone number?
questions.translation: Số điện thoại là gì?

questions.questionText: Who is the instructor?
questions.translation: Ai là người hướng dẫn?

questions.questionText: What must students bring?
questions.translation: Học viên phải mang theo gì?

questions.questionText: What is the minimum age requirement?
questions.translation: Yêu cầu tuổi tối thiểu là bao nhiêu?

questions.questionText: What happens in the final week?
questions.translation: Điều gì xảy ra trong tuần cuối cùng?

parts.title: Part 2: Monologue about theater history
parts.instructions: Listen to the monologue and answer questions 11-20.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 2. You will hear a monologue about theater history. Look at questions 11 to 20. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 20.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Welcome to today's lecture on the history of Western theater. Theater as we know it began in ancient Greece around the 5th century BCE. The earliest surviving Greek tragedies were written by Aeschylus, Sophocles, and Euripides. These plays were performed in large outdoor amphitheaters that could seat up to 14,000 spectators. During the medieval period, theater moved to churches and town squares, with performances often depicting biblical stories. The Renaissance brought about the Elizabethan era, famous for William Shakespeare who wrote 37 plays between 1589 and 1613. The Globe Theatre in London, built in 1599, could hold 3,000 people. Modern theater developed in the 19th century with the works of Henrik Ibsen and Anton Chekhov, who introduced realism. Today's theater continues to evolve with experimental forms and digital technology.

questions.questionText: When did Western theater begin?
questions.translation: Kịch phương Tây bắt đầu từ khi nào?

questions.questionText: Which playwright is NOT mentioned from ancient Greece?
questions.translation: Nhà soạn kịch nào KHÔNG được đề cập từ Hy Lạp cổ đại?

questions.questionText: How many people could Greek amphitheaters hold?
questions.translation: Các nhà hát hình bán nguyệt Hy Lạp có thể chứa bao nhiêu người?

questions.questionText: Where were medieval plays performed?
questions.translation: Các vở kịch thời trung cổ được biểu diễn ở đâu?

questions.questionText: How many plays did Shakespeare write?
questions.translation: Shakespeare đã viết bao nhiêu vở kịch?

questions.questionText: When was the Globe Theatre built?
questions.translation: Nhà hát Globe được xây dựng khi nào?

questions.questionText: What was the capacity of the Globe Theatre?
questions.translation: Sức chứa của Nhà hát Globe là bao nhiêu?

questions.questionText: Which century saw the development of realism?
questions.translation: Thế kỷ nào chứng kiến sự phát triển của chủ nghĩa hiện thực?

questions.questionText: Which playwright introduced realism?
questions.translation: Nhà soạn kịch nào đã giới thiệu chủ nghĩa hiện thực?

questions.questionText: What is mentioned about modern theater?
questions.translation: Điều gì được đề cập về sân khấu hiện đại?

parts.title: Part 3: Conversation between drama students
parts.instructions: Listen to the conversation and answer questions 21-30.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 3. You will hear a conversation between two drama students. Look at questions 21 to 30. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 30.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Tom, I'm really struggling with my character analysis for our upcoming play. How do you approach understanding your character's motivation?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Well Emma, I always start by reading the script at least three times. The first time for the overall story, second for my character's journey, and third for specific details about their personality.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: That makes sense. Our professor mentioned using Stanislavski's method. Do you follow that?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Yes, I find his system very helpful. The 'magic if' technique is particularly useful - asking yourself 'what if I were in this situation?' It helps create authentic emotional responses.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: I've been trying that, but I still find some scenes challenging. Like the confrontation scene in Act 2 - my character has to show anger, but I'm not naturally an angry person.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: For emotional recall, think of a time when you felt genuinely angry about something. It doesn't have to be the same situation, just the same intensity of emotion. The key is to find a personal connection.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: That's helpful. What about physicality? How important is body language in character development?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Extremely important! About 70% of communication is non-verbal. I always work on my character's walk, posture, and gestures before I even memorize lines. It helps internalize the character.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: I'll try that approach. Thank you, Tom! This has been really insightful.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: You're welcome, Emma. Remember, acting is about truthfulness. If you believe in your character, the audience will too.

questions.questionText: What is Emma struggling with?
questions.translation: Emma đang gặp khó khăn với điều gì?

questions.questionText: How many times does Tom read the script initially?
questions.translation: Tom đọc kịch bản bao nhiêu lần ban đầu?

questions.questionText: Which acting method is mentioned?
questions.translation: Phương pháp diễn xuất nào được đề cập?

questions.questionText: What is the 'magic if' technique?
options: B) Asking 'what if I were in this situation'
questions.translation: Kỹ thuật 'phép màu nếu' là gì?

questions.questionText: Which scene does Emma find challenging?
questions.translation: Cảnh nào Emma thấy thách thức?

questions.questionText: What does Tom suggest for emotional recall?
questions.translation: Tom đề xuất gì cho việc hồi tưởng cảm xúc?

questions.questionText: What percentage of communication is non-verbal according to Tom?
questions.translation: Theo Tom, bao nhiêu phần trăm giao tiếp là phi ngôn ngữ?

questions.questionText: What does Tom work on before memorizing lines?
questions.translation: Tom làm việc trên cái gì trước khi ghi nhớ lời thoại?

questions.questionText: What is the key to believable acting according to Tom?
questions.translation: Theo Tom, chìa khóa cho diễn xuất đáng tin cậy là gì?

questions.questionText: How does Emma feel about Tom's advice?
questions.translation: Emma cảm thấy thế nào về lời khuyên của Tom?

parts.title: Part 4: Lecture on acting techniques
parts.instructions: Listen to the lecture and answer questions 31-40.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 4. You will hear a lecture on acting techniques. Look at questions 31 to 40. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Today we'll explore three major approaches to acting that have shaped modern theater and film. First, the Stanislavski System, developed by Russian actor Konstantin Stanislavski in the early 20th century. This method emphasizes psychological realism and emotional truth. Actors use techniques like emotional memory and the 'magic if' to create believable characters. Second, we have Bertolt Brecht's Epic Theater, which emerged in Germany during the 1920s. Unlike Stanislavski, Brecht wanted to create distance between the audience and the performance through techniques like direct address and placards. His goal was to make audiences think critically about social issues rather than emotionally identify with characters. Finally, the Meisner Technique, developed by American actor Sanford Meisner in the 1930s, focuses on spontaneous reaction and 'living truthfully under imaginary circumstances.' Meisner emphasized listening and responding authentically to other actors rather than pre-planning reactions. Each approach offers valuable tools, and many contemporary actors blend elements from multiple techniques to create their unique acting style.

questions.questionText: How many acting approaches are discussed?
questions.translation: Có bao nhiêu phương pháp diễn xuất được thảo luận?

questions.questionText: Who developed the Stanislavski System?
questions.translation: Ai đã phát triển Hệ thống Stanislavski?

questions.questionText: What does the Stanislavski System emphasize?
questions.translation: Hệ thống Stanislavski nhấn mạnh điều gì?

questions.questionText: Which technique uses emotional memory?
questions.translation: Kỹ thuật nào sử dụng trí nhớ cảm xúc?

questions.questionText: Where did Epic Theater originate?
questions.translation: Kịch Epic bắt nguồn từ đâu?

questions.questionText: What was Brecht's main goal?
questions.translation: Mục tiêu chính của Brecht là gì?

questions.questionText: Which technique uses direct address to the audience?
questions.translation: Kỹ thuật nào sử dụng việc nói chuyện trực tiếp với khán giả?

questions.questionText: Who developed the Meisner Technique?
questions.translation: Ai đã phát triển Kỹ thuật Meisner?

questions.questionText: What does the Meisner Technique focus on?
questions.translation: Kỹ thuật Meisner tập trung vào điều gì?

questions.questionText: What do contemporary actors often do?
questions.translation: Các diễn viên đương đại thường làm gì?

passages.title: Passage 1: The History of Method Acting
passages.content: Method acting is a range of training and rehearsal techniques that seek to encourage sincere and emotionally expressive performances. It was first popularized by the Group Theatre in the 1930s and later developed by Lee Strasberg at the Actors Studio in New York. The approach draws from the system developed by Russian theatre practitioner Konstantin Stanislavski. Strasberg's method requires actors to draw upon their own emotions and memories to connect with their characters. This technique became famous through actors like Marlon Brando, James Dean, and Marilyn Monroe. While some critics argue that method acting can be psychologically damaging, its proponents believe it creates unparalleled authenticity. The technique involves extensive character research, emotional memory exercises, and sometimes extreme physical transformations. Many contemporary acting schools continue to teach variations of method acting, though often with modifications to address concerns about actors' mental health. The legacy of method acting continues to influence film and theater worldwide, with many award-winning performances attributed to this approach.

questions.questionText: Where was method acting first popularized?
Who further developed method acting at the Actors Studio?
Which Russian practitioner influenced method acting?
What does method acting require actors to draw upon?
options: B) Their own emotions and memories

questions.questionText: Which actor is NOT mentioned as famous for method acting?
What concern do some critics have about method acting?
What do proponents believe about method acting?
What does method acting sometimes involve?
How do contemporary acting schools often teach method acting?
options: B) With modifications for mental health

questions.questionText: What continues to be influenced by method acting?

passages.title: Passage 2: Shakespeare's Globe Theatre
passages.content: The Globe Theatre was a theatre in London associated with William Shakespeare. It was built in 1599 by Shakespeare's playing company, the Lord Chamberlain's Men, and was destroyed by fire on 29 June 1613. A second Globe Theatre was built on the same site by June 1614 and closed in 1642. The modern reconstruction, named 'Shakespeare's Globe', opened in 1997 approximately 230 meters from the original site. The original Globe could accommodate up to 3,000 spectators and was famous for its circular shape and open-air design. Plays were performed in the afternoon to take advantage of natural light. The theatre had three levels of seating and a yard where 'groundlings' (commoners) stood for a penny. The stage extended into the yard, creating an intimate relationship between actors and audience. Sound effects were created using cannons, bells, and other props, which ironically caused the fire that destroyed the first Globe when a cannon misfired during a performance of Henry VIII. Today, Shakespeare's Globe attracts visitors from around the world and continues to stage productions using original practices, including period costumes and minimal sets.

questions.questionText: When was the original Globe Theatre built?
What destroyed the first Globe Theatre?
When did the modern reconstruction open?
How many spectators could the original Globe accommodate?
Why were plays performed in the afternoon?
What were 'groundlings'?
How much did groundlings pay?
What caused the fire that destroyed the first Globe?
During which play did the fire occur?
What does the modern Globe continue to use?

passages.title: Passage 3: The Evolution of Women in Theater
passages.content: The role of women in theater has evolved significantly throughout history. In ancient Greek theater, all roles were played by men, including female characters who were portrayed by male actors wearing masks. During the Renaissance, women were still banned from the stage in England, leading to young boys playing female roles in Shakespeare's plays. The first professional actresses appeared in Italy during the 16th century in commedia dell'arte troupes. In England, women finally began appearing on stage after the Restoration of Charles II in 1660, with Margaret Hughes often credited as the first professional actress on the English stage. The 19th century saw the rise of famous actresses like Sarah Bernhardt, known as 'the divine Sarah,' who achieved international stardom. The 20th century brought further progress with women not only acting but also writing, directing, and producing plays. Playwrights like Lillian Hellman and Caryl Churchill broke barriers with powerful works addressing social issues. Today, women hold prominent positions across all aspects of theater, though gender inequality and representation issues persist. Contemporary movements continue to advocate for equal opportunities and diverse representation both on stage and behind the scenes in theaters worldwide.

questions.questionText: Who played female roles in ancient Greek theater?
Who played female roles in Shakespeare's time?
Where did the first professional actresses appear?
When did women begin appearing on stage in England?
Who is often credited as the first professional English actress?
Which 19th century actress was known as 'the divine Sarah'?
What additional roles did women take in the 20th century?
Which playwright is mentioned as breaking barriers?
What issues still persist today according to the passage?
What do contemporary movements advocate for?

tasks.title: Writing Task 1
tasks.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on this task. The chart below shows the percentage of theater attendance by age group in London between 2010 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.
tasks.description: You will be presented with a graph, table, chart or diagram and asked to describe, summarize or explain the information in your own words. You may be asked to describe and explain data, describe the stages of a process, how something works or describe an object or event.

sampleData.title: Theater Attendance by Age Group in London (2010-2020)

tasks.title: Writing Task 2
tasks.instructions: You should spend about 40 minutes on this task. Some people believe that acting is a natural talent that cannot be taught, while others argue that acting can be learned through proper training and practice. Discuss both these views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.
tasks.description: You will be presented with a point of view, argument or problem. You need to write a discursive essay in response. This means you must discuss the topic from different perspectives and include your own opinion.
keyPoints: Arguments for natural talent in acting
Arguments for acting as a learned skill

questions.questionText: The actor _____ his lines perfectly in yesterday's rehearsal.
questions.explanation: Past simple for completed action in the past
questions.translation: Diễn viên đã đọc lời thoại hoàn hảo trong buổi diễn tập hôm qua.

questions.questionText: If I _____ a famous actor, I would work in Hollywood.
questions.explanation: Second conditional uses 'were' for all subjects
questions.translation: Nếu tôi là một diễn viên nổi tiếng, tôi sẽ làm việc ở Hollywood.

questions.questionText: She _____ acting classes for two years now.
questions.explanation: Present perfect continuous for ongoing action
questions.translation: Cô ấy đã tham gia lớp học diễn xuất được hai năm rồi.

questions.questionText: The play must _____ by 8 PM sharp.
questions.explanation: Passive voice with modal verb
questions.translation: Vở kịch phải được bắt đầu lúc 8 giờ tối chính xác.

questions.questionText: I wish I _____ more confidence on stage.
questions.explanation: 'I wish' + past simple for present unreal situations
questions.translation: Tôi ước tôi có nhiều tự tin hơn trên sân khấu.

questions.questionText: _____ is your favorite Shakespeare play?
questions.explanation: Use 'What' for open questions about things
questions.translation: Vở kịch Shakespeare yêu thích của bạn là gì?

questions.questionText: The director told us that the audition _____ postponed.
questions.explanation: Past perfect in reported speech
questions.translation: Đạo diễn nói với chúng tôi rằng buổi thử vai đã bị hoãn.

questions.questionText: Neither of the actors _____ available for the matinee.
questions.explanation: 'Neither' is singular and takes singular verb
questions.translation: Không diễn viên nào có mặt cho buổi biểu diễn chiều.

questions.questionText: She suggested _____ the scene one more time.
questions.explanation: 'Suggest' is followed by gerund (-ing form)
questions.translation: Cô ấy đề nghị diễn tập cảnh đó thêm một lần nữa.

questions.questionText: The theater, _____ was built in 1920, needs renovation.
questions.explanation: Use 'which' for things in non-defining relative clauses
questions.translation: Nhà hát, cái được xây dựng năm 1920, cần được tu sửa.

questions.questionText: By next month, we _____ this play for six months.
questions.explanation: Future perfect for actions completed before a future time
questions.translation: Đến tháng sau, chúng tôi sẽ diễn tập vở kịch này được sáu tháng.

questions.questionText: The lead actor rarely _____ his cues.
questions.explanation: Present simple with frequency adverbs
questions.translation: Diễn viên chính hiếm khi bỏ lỡ các điểm nhấn của mình.

questions.questionText: Would you mind _____ your voice a little?
questions.explanation: 'Would you mind' + gerund (-ing)
questions.translation: Bạn có phiền hạ giọng một chút không?

questions.questionText: The understudy was made _____ the lead role.
questions.explanation: Passive causative 'be made' + to infinitive
questions.translation: Diễn viên dự bị bị buộc phải đóng vai chính.

questions.questionText: Despite _____ ill, she performed brilliantly.
questions.explanation: 'Despite' is followed by gerund or noun
questions.translation: Mặc dù bị ốm, cô ấy đã biểu diễn xuất sắc.

questions.questionText: The stage _____ we performed was very small.
questions.explanation: Use 'where' for places in relative clauses
questions.translation: Sân khấu nơi chúng tôi biểu diễn rất nhỏ.

questions.questionText: No sooner had the curtain risen _____ the lights went out.
questions.explanation: 'No sooner... than' for immediate sequence
questions.translation: Ngay sau khi màn kéo lên thì đèn tắt.

questions.questionText: She acts _____ than anyone else in the cast.
questions.explanation: Comparative adverbs for comparing two things
questions.translation: Cô ấy diễn xuất thuyết phục hơn bất kỳ ai khác trong dàn diễn viên.

questions.questionText: I would rather _____ comedy than tragedy.
questions.explanation: 'Would rather' + base form of verb
questions.translation: Tôi thà diễn hài kịch hơn là bi kịch.

questions.questionText: It is high time you _____ your audition piece.
questions.explanation: 'It is high time' + past simple
questions.translation: Đã đến lúc bạn chọn phần thử vai của mình.

metadata.author: IELTS Practice System
metadata.notes: IELTS test with acting and theater theme covering Listening (4 parts, 40 questions), Reading (3 passages, 30 questions), Writing (2 tasks), and Grammar (20 questions).
